competition: 74th NMO Selection Tests for BMO and IMO
topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Counting two ways
Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Sequences and Series > Sums and products
Algebra > Equations and Inequalities > Linear and quadratic inequalities
Algebra > Equations and Inequalities > Combinatorial optimization
prompt:
Fix an integer $n \ge 2$. Consider $n$ real numbers $a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$, not all equal, and let
$$
d = d(a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n) = \max_{1 \le i < j \le n} |a_i - a_j|
$$
and
$$
s = s(a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n) = \sum_{1 \le i < j \le n} |a_i - a_j|.
$$

Determine, in terms of $n$, the smallest and the largest values the quotient $s/d$ may achieve.
response_text:
Let
\[
m=\min_i a_i,\qquad M=\max_i a_i,
\]
so since the numbers are not all equal,
\[
d=M-m>0.
\]
We want the extremal values of
\[
\frac{s}{d},\qquad s=\sum_{i<j}|a_i-a_j|.
\]

It is convenient to sort the numbers:
\[
x_1\le x_2\le \cdots \le x_n,
\]
so \(x_1=m,\ x_n=M,\ d=x_n-x_1\). Then
\[
s=\sum_{i<j}(x_j-x_i).
\]

## 1) Minimum of \(s/d\)

Since among the terms of \(s\) there is the pair \((1,n)\), we have
\[
s\ge x_n-x_1=d.
\]
Hence
\[
\frac{s}{d}\ge 1.
\]

This lower bound is attained: take
\[
a_1=\cdots=a_{n-1}=0,\qquad a_n=1.
\]
Then \(d=1\), and the only nonzero pairwise differences are the \(n-1\) pairs involving \(a_n\), so actually this gives \(s=n-1\), not 1. So we need a sharper check.

For any \(k\) numbers equal to \(m\) and \(n-k\) equal to \(M\), we get
\[
s=k(n-k)d.
\]
This is minimized for \(k=1\) or \(k=n-1\), giving
\[
s=(n-1)d.
\]
So certainly \(\frac{s}{d}\le n-1\) is achievable.

Now prove this is the true minimum. For each interior index \(i=1,\dots,n-1\),
\[
x_n-x_1=(x_{i+1}-x_i)+\text{other nonnegative gaps},
\]
and summing all pairwise distances in the standard gap form gives
\[
s=\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} i(n-i)(x_{i+1}-x_i).
\]
Since \(i(n-i)\ge n-1\) for all \(i=1,\dots,n-1\),
\[
s\ge (n-1)\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}(x_{i+1}-x_i)=(n-1)(x_n-x_1)=(n-1)d.
\]
Thus
\[
\frac{s}{d}\ge n-1,
\]
with equality when only one gap is nonzero, i.e. all numbers take only the two extreme values and one of those values occurs exactly once.

So the minimum is
\[
\boxed{n-1}.
\]

## 2) Maximum of \(s/d\)

Again use
\[
s=\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} i(n-i)(x_{i+1}-x_i),
\qquad
d=\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}(x_{i+1}-x_i).
\]
Set
\[
g_i=x_{i+1}-x_i\ge 0.
\]
Then
\[
\frac{s}{d}
=
\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} i(n-i)g_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} g_i}.
\]
This is a weighted average of the numbers \(i(n-i)\), so it lies between their minimum and maximum. Hence
\[
\frac{s}{d}\le \max_{1\le i\le n-1} i(n-i).
\]
The maximum of \(i(n-i)\) is attained in the middle:
\[
\max_{1\le i\le n-1} i(n-i)=\left\lfloor \frac{n^2}{4}\right\rfloor.
\]
Therefore
\[
\frac{s}{d}\le \left\lfloor \frac{n^2}{4}\right\rfloor.
\]

This bound is attainable by making only one gap nonzero, at an index \(i\) maximizing \(i(n-i)\): take
\[
x_1=\cdots=x_i=0,\qquad x_{i+1}=\cdots=x_n=1,
\]
with \(i=\lfloor n/2\rfloor\) or \(i=\lceil n/2\rceil\). Then \(d=1\) and
\[
s=i(n-i)=\left\lfloor \frac{n^2}{4}\right\rfloor.
\]

So the maximum is
\[
\boxed{\left\lfloor \frac{n^2}{4}\right\rfloor}.
\]

Final answer: \(\displaystyle \min \frac{s}{d}=n-1,\qquad \max \frac{s}{d}=\left\lfloor \frac{n^2}{4}\right\rfloor.\)
